paragraphs.body: My Celtic heritage has helped me to discover who I am. Through the songs and stories of my grandparents, I’ve learned about my roots and ultimately been shaped by them. My family is of Scottish and Irish Celtic ancestry. The article, “Who Were the Celts?” written and edited by the HISTORY.com editors Amanda Onion, Missy Sullivan, Matt Mullen, and Christian Zapata, provides an insightful overview of the Celts and their lasting impact on modern societies. It traces the origins of the Celts, a diverse group of tribes, spread across Europe, known for their rich culture, famous artistry, and warrior spirit. Learning about my genetic Celtic and cultural background has been impactful in the way I see myself in the world.

annotations.text: Learning about my genetic Celtic and cultural background has been impactful in the way I see myself in the world.
annotations.comment: ditto my comment above :)

annotations.text: My Celtic heritage has helped me to discover who I am. Through the songs and stories of my grandparents, I’ve learned about my roots and ultimately been shaped by them. My family is of Scottish and Irish Celtic ancestry.
annotations.comment: Remember to stick to "they say" in this summary (and use the summary templates/writing moves we learned in class). You can introduce your own ideas later in the essay--after you've set the context and let your reader know which "conversation" your essay is entering.

paragraphs.body: The Celtic people and culture are much more varied and widespread than most people know. Celtic culture crossed many land masses and societies, all sharing common Celtic roots. The article states, “Several tribes made up the larger population of the Celtic people. Indeed, the Gaels, Gauls, Britons, Irish, and Galatians were all Celtic tribes.” Their culture and traditions spread across Europe, and far from the British Isles where they’re generally considered confined.

Many languages trace their origins to the Celts. The native tongue in Wales is Welsh and is still taught in Welsh schools today. A small percentage of the Cornwall population speaks Cornish. In Scotland and Ireland, the Celtic languages that are still spoken today by some people are Scots Gaelic and Irish Gaelic. Many of these languages are slowly being forgotten due to the lack of their appearance and use in modern society.

annotations.text: The native tongue in Wales is Welsh and is still taught in Welsh schools today. A small percentage of the Cornwall
annotations.comment: I'm not exactly sure how this idea is connected to the Celts?

paragraphs.body: The Roman Empire labeled the Celts as “Galli,” meaning barbarians. Yet in truth, “The Celts... were anything but barbarians.” As stated in the article their rich culture, passed down through generations, is evidence of their sophistication. Despite Rome's aggressive campaign to suppress and eliminate Celtic culture, killing thousands and erasing much of their influence across Europe, the Celts persevered. Those who survived established a homeland in modern day Britain, allowing their cultural traditions to live on. Today, many of these traditions continue to thrive in Wales, Ireland, and Scotland, keeping the Celtic heritage alive.

annotations.text: The Roman Empire labeled the Celts as “Galli,” meaning barbarians.

paragraphs.body: Celtic people and culture are important to me because I am of Scottish and Irish ancestry, I have been surrounded by the influences of Celtic culture for my entire life. I am not a respected warrior like the ancient Celts, and I don’t speak their language. I am an American regardless of my Scottish and Irish heritage. However, Celtic traditions have played a role in my life and I acknowledge and cherish those aspects of myself.

annotations.text: I am not a respected warrior like the ancient Celts, and I don’t speak their language. I am an American regardless of my Scottish and Irish heritage. However, Celtic traditions have played a role in my life and I acknowledge and cherish those aspects of myself.
annotations.comment: Nice move to introduce the personal connection~

paragraphs.body: My whole life, my maternal grandmother, Pricilla McNulty, has expressed our family’s heritage in various ways. After her husband, my mom's father, Arthur McNulty passed away, she replaced her wedding ring with an Irish Celtic knot ring as a tribute to his roots as a proud Irish American. Beyond her fondness for plaid blankets and scarves, one of my most cherished memories is of her singing voice. When I was younger, my grandmother would sing me lullabies in Irish Gaelic.

I spoke with my grandmother recently to better understand her perspective of our culture. I learned that it isn’t from her side of the family, but from my grandfather’s side of the family, she told me she loved him because of his Irish eyes, which she said always had a certain sparkle. Besides loving him for his Irish eyes, she also told me about the many ways he showed he cared about his community. Learning more about my grandfather was important to me because he passed away before I was born. He was a priest at Calvary Episcopal Church but when he was younger, he was a bit of a rebellious teenager. In college, he was the president of the student government and as a project, they did a study on how the college was run to try to better the school. This study was frowned upon by the administration, for obvious reasons, but it was also very progressive for the time, so they made it into the local city newspaper. Later in life, in his role as a clergyman, my grandfather marched alongside Martin Luther King, Jr. in Selma, Alabama. His dedication to community and rebellious streak is something his fighting Irish ancestors would be proud of.

annotations.text: told me she loved him because of his Irish eyes, which she said always had a certain sparkle.
annotations.comment: beautiful :) (but watch out for comma splices!)

annotations.text: Later in life, in his role as a clergyman, my grandfather marched alongside Martin Luther King, Jr. in Selma, Alabama. His dedication to community and rebellious streak is something his fighting Irish ancestors would be proud of.
annotations.comment: So cool :)

paragraphs.body: Learning these things about my family is inspiring. Due to this, I have a strong connection to activism. My grandmother was also an activist when she was younger. As a college student, she tutored children because where she lived, they didn’t have public kindergartens, only private ones. Because of this, she volunteered to teach the less advantaged children herself. After a long time working with this group, she was eventually offered a position at one of the private kindergartens. She said she would only work there if they offered the other kids scholarships. My grandmother is still an activist today. She works to fight against gerrymandering. She has spoken to me about her passion for it and how important it is to her. This ability to look out for those less fortunate than yourself can be seen in the tens of thousands of Irish and Scottish men and women who made sacrifices to better their lives and the lives of those around them by rising against oppression where they saw it in their lives.

annotations.text: This ability to look out for those less fortunate than yourself can be seen in the tens of thousands of Irish and Scottish men and women who made sacrifices to better their lives and the lives of those around them by rising against oppression where they saw it in their lives.

paragraphs.body: The impact of my family's American, Scottish, and Irish heritage on me is invaluable. I love this identity and find it truly special. What I cherish even more is my family itself. I admire the spirit of activism passed down to me by my grandparents and specifically how my grandfather embodied it in his life when he was alive. The Celts were a diverse group of talented people, who through migration, spread across Europe and then many to the Americas. My family is among those impacted by Celtic traditions. These Celtic influences are meaningful to me and reflect centuries of cultural evolution. As highlighted in the article, “Who Were the Celts?” by the editors of History.com, understanding our past can deeply shape our perspective on the future.
